refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. The refined FR focuses on the core functional need: translating mission context into measurable performance targets. This remains solution-neutral and avoids prescribing how derivation, recording, or management are realized.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints), the system produces a documented set of performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that are traceable to input parameters and accepted by the Site Manager and Safety Manager.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall accept worksite mission context inputs (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety policies, environmental constraints).
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall compute performance requirements (e.g., cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) from worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain a record of derived performance requirements and their source context.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall enable updates to performance requirements when worksite mission context changes.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a mission-context capture module that accepts worksite inputs (safety plan, operational policies, environmental conditions) and applies a rule-based inference engine to derive performance targets; maintain a versioned baseline repository with change tracking.

DP-B: Establish a hierarchical performance requirement decomposition process where worksite mission context is mapped to system-level performance targets through a documented traceability matrix; store all versions in a configuration management system.

DP-C: Design a parameterized performance model that accepts worksite mission context as input variables and computes performance targets via a calibrated mathematical model; log all model inputs, outputs, and calibration events.

DP-D: Create a manual performance requirement specification workflow where domain experts review worksite context and document performance targets in a structured template; implement a change control board to approve and version all updates.

DP-E: Implement a hybrid approach combining automated context parsing (DP-A logic) with expert validation gates (DP-D oversight) to derive performance targets; maintain dual-layer traceability (automated derivation + expert approval) and full audit history.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-E: Implement a hybrid approach combining automated context parsing with expert validation gates to derive performance targets; maintain dual-layer traceability (automated derivation + expert approval) and full audit history.

potential_sources: Coupling between context capture module and traceability matrix if schema changes are not versioned independently.
Coupling between performance model and baseline repository if model updates are not decoupled from historical baseline storage.
Coupling between expert validation workflow and automated inference if approval logic is hardcoded rather than configurable.
coupling_check.notes: DP-E mitigates coupling by separating concerns: (1) automated derivation logic is isolated from (2) expert validation gates, and (3) traceability/audit is orthogonal to both. The dual-layer approach allows independent evolution of inference rules and approval policies. Risk: if validation gate criteria are tightly bound to specific performance model assumptions, decoupling may be compromised. Mitigation: define validation gate criteria as independent configuration, not embedded in model code.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context inputs are available in a structured or semi-structured format (e.g., safety plan document, operational policy database). RISK: If inputs are unstructured narrative text, automated parsing will require NLP or manual pre-processing, increasing complexity and error rate.

ASSUMPTION: Expert validators are available and responsive to approve derived performance targets within acceptable lead time. RISK: If validation becomes a bottleneck, the system may not meet operational agility requirements.

ASSUMPTION: Performance targets derived from mission context are deterministic or have well-defined uncertainty bounds. RISK: If mission context is ambiguous or contradictory, the system may produce conflicting or non-traceable targets.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are maintained in a controlled configuration. RISK: If policies change frequently without formal change control, traceability to performance targets may become stale or incorrect.
ASSUMPTION: Baseline and calibration history is required for compliance or audit purposes. RISK: If retention policy is not defined, storage and retrieval of historical data may become unmanageable.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR-NGHE-005 (refined): The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context via a hybrid process that (1) automatically parses worksite inputs (safety plan, operational policies, environmental conditions) to generate candidate performance targets, (2) subjects candidates to expert validation and approval, (3) produces a documented, versioned set of performance targets with full traceability to source inputs and approval records, and (4) maintains a queryable history of all baselines, calibration updates, and policy changes. Acceptance criteria: (a) Every performance target is traceable to at least one worksite input and one expert approval record. (b) All baseline and calibration updates are logged with timestamp, actor, and rationale. (c) System supports rollback to any prior baseline version. (d) Traceability reports are generated on demand and include derivation chain and validation evidence.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for performance target derivation, validation gate management, dual-layer traceability (automated + expert approval), and audit history maintenance for safety-critical performance parameters.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational context data (vehicle state, environmental conditions, mission parameters) to feed automated context parsing for performance target derivation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives task specifications and returns validated performance targets derived from hybrid analysis; enables expert validation gate feedback loop.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits audit trail events, validation gate decisions, and traceability records documenting both automated derivation and expert approval steps.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Safety Services is the natural owner of performance target validation, expert gates, and audit trails. Automated context parsing and dual-layer traceability are core safety concerns. Minor diffusion: Telemetry ingestion and Tasking integration require external interfaces, but logic remains cohesive within Safety Services.

Poor fit: Vehicle module typically owns physical state and actuation, not safety validation logic or expert approval workflows. Performance target derivation and audit history are safety-domain concerns, not vehicle-domain concerns. High diffusion: expert validation gates and traceability would leak into Vehicle, violating separation of concerns.

Poor fit: Teleoperation owns remote control and operator interface, not safety-critical performance target derivation or expert validation. Audit history and dual-layer traceability belong in safety domain, not teleoperation domain. High diffusion: expert gates and validation logic would be misplaced in operator-facing module.

Poor fit: NGHE Solution is likely a top-level system container, not a specific functional module. Placing DP-E here would lack clear ownership and accountability. Performance target derivation requires dedicated safety expertise and governance, not system-level aggregation. High diffusion: logic would be scattered across multiple sub-modules without clear boundary.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the appropriate IM boundary because: (1) performance target validation is inherently a safety-critical function requiring expert oversight; (2) dual-layer traceability (automated + expert approval) aligns with safety governance patterns; (3) audit history maintenance is a core safety compliance requirement; (4) expert validation gates are safety domain responsibilities; (5) automated context parsing for safety targets belongs in the safety module, not vehicle or teleoperation modules.
containment_risks: Risk: Telemetry interface may require high-frequency polling or streaming, creating latency sensitivity in Safety Services if not designed for async processing.
Risk: Expert validation gates introduce human-in-the-loop delays; if performance targets must be derived in real-time, gate latency could become a bottleneck.
Risk: Audit history growth could become unbounded; retention policy and storage strategy must be defined to prevent Safety Services from becoming a data sink.
Risk: Dual-layer traceability requires tight coupling between automated derivation logic and expert approval workflow; if these evolve independently, maintenance burden increases.
Risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation modules need to query or override performance targets, Safety Services becomes a critical dependency; interface contracts must be stable.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. Acceptance criteria explicitly require 'documented set of performance targets traceable to worksite inputs' and 'history of baselines/calibration updates.' Safety Services IM boundary directly owns audit trail emission (SafetyAlert interface) and dual-layer traceability maintenance, making verification of FR compliance straightforward through audit log inspection and traceability record validation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. FR-CR-NGHE-005 maps unambiguously to Safety Services: performance target derivation (core responsibility), validation gate management (expert approval workflow), dual-layer traceability (automated + expert approval), and audit history (SafetyAlert output). No ambiguity in ownership or accountability.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-E (hybrid approach combining automated context parsing + expert validation gates with dual-layer traceability and audit history) directly and sufficiently addresses FR-CR-NGHE-005 (derive performance requirements from worksite mission context). The DP specifies both the mechanism (hybrid automated+expert) and the governance layer (dual-layer traceability, audit history), satisfying the functional requirement's intent to ground performance targets in mission context with documented provenance.

diffusion_risks: LATENCY SENSITIVITY: Telemetry interface ingestion at high frequency could create real-time processing bottleneck in Safety Services if async buffering and batch processing are not explicitly designed. Recommend defining maximum acceptable latency SLA for context-to-target derivation pipeline.

HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP DELAY: Expert validation gates introduce non-deterministic approval latency. If performance targets must be derived and applied within hard real-time bounds (e.g., <100ms), gate approval could become a critical path blocker. Recommend defining gate timeout policy and fallback behavior (e.g., conservative defaults pending approval).

AUDIT HISTORY UNBOUNDED GROWTH: SafetyAlert emission of every derivation step and approval decision could generate high-volume event stream. Without retention policy, Safety Services audit storage could become a data sink. Recommend defining retention windows (e.g., 90-day rolling window) and archival strategy.

TIGHT COUPLING BETWEEN AUTOMATED DERIVATION AND EXPERT WORKFLOW: Dual-layer traceability requires synchronized state between automated parsing logic and expert approval state machine. If these evolve independently (e.g., new parsing algorithms vs. new approval workflows), maintenance burden and regression risk increase. Recommend defining explicit contract/interface between derivation engine and approval workflow.

CRITICAL DEPENDENCY FOR VEHICLE/TELEOPERATION: If Vehicle or Teleoperation modules query Safety Services for performance targets or attempt to override them, Safety Services becomes a single point of failure. Recommend defining fallback performance targets and circuit-breaker pattern for interface failures.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT CONTAINMENT (0.92 score justified). Safety Services is the natural and appropriate IM boundary for DP-E. Rationale: (1) Performance target validation is inherently safety-critical and requires expert governance—belongs in safety domain, not vehicle actuation or teleoperation control. (2) Dual-layer traceability (automated + expert approval) is a safety compliance pattern; audit history is a core safety governance artifact. (3) Expert validation gates are safety domain responsibilities by definition. (4) Automated context parsing for safety-critical targets belongs in safety module to maintain separation of concerns. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable: Telemetry and Tasking interfaces are external dependencies, not logic leakage. Safety Services correctly owns the derivation and validation logic; it consumes telemetry and produces validated targets via Tasking—this is proper interface design, not diffusion.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1 - LATENCY & REAL-TIME REQUIREMENTS: Explicitly define maximum acceptable latency for context-to-target derivation (e.g., 'performance targets must be available within 500ms of worksite context update'). If hard real-time constraints exist, specify async buffering strategy and batch processing windows in Safety Services design.

REVISION 2 - EXPERT GATE TIMEOUT POLICY: Define expert validation gate timeout behavior. Specify: (a) maximum wait time for expert approval before fallback action triggers, (b) fallback performance targets (conservative or nominal), (c) escalation procedure if gate timeout occurs repeatedly. Add to DP-E specification.

REVISION 3 - AUDIT HISTORY RETENTION & ARCHIVAL: Define retention policy for audit trail events emitted via SafetyAlert. Specify: (a) retention window (e.g., 90 days hot storage, 1 year cold archive), (b) archival destination (e.g., external audit database), (c) purge/deletion criteria. Add to DP-E specification to prevent unbounded growth.

REVISION 4 - DERIVATION-APPROVAL INTERFACE CONTRACT: Formalize the interface between automated context parsing engine and expert approval workflow. Specify: (a) input schema for derivation engine (parsed context), (b) output schema for approval workflow (approved targets + metadata), (c) versioning strategy if either component evolves. Add to Safety Services module design document.

REVISION 5 - FALLBACK & CIRCUIT-BREAKER PATTERN: Define fallback behavior if Safety Services becomes unavailable or unresponsive. Specify: (a) default/conservative performance targets for Vehicle and Teleoperation to use, (b) circuit-breaker thresholds (e.g., 3 consecutive timeouts), (c) recovery procedure. Add to external interface contracts (Tasking, SafetyAlert).

REVISION 6 - TRACEABILITY RECORD SCHEMA: Formalize the structure of dual-layer traceability records emitted via SafetyAlert. Specify: (a) automated derivation step metadata (input context, parsing algorithm, derived targets), (b) expert approval metadata (approver identity, approval timestamp, approval rationale), (c) version/audit trail linkage. Ensure schema supports acceptance criterion 'documented set of performance targets traceable to worksite inputs.'

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence, indicating the functional requirement is well-formed and the design approach is sound. However, the DP-IM gate has identified six critical diffusion risks that are not merely implementation details—they are architectural constraints that must be explicitly captured in the DP specification before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. The risks span latency sensitivity, human-in-the-loop delays, unbounded audit growth, tight coupling, single-point-of-failure dependencies, and traceability schema formalization. These are not problems with the current FR or DP logic; they are gaps in the DP specification that must be closed to ensure the design is realizable and maintainable. Revising DP-E to incorporate the six recommended revisions will harden the design and provide a clearer contract for downstream sub-FR derivation. Once DP-E is revised, sub-FR decomposition can proceed with confidence that the parent DP is fully specified.

revise.why: DP-E (hybrid automated context parsing + expert validation gates with dual-layer traceability and audit history) is logically sound and correctly addresses FR-CR-NGHE-005. However, the specification is incomplete with respect to non-functional constraints and architectural patterns required for safe, scalable, and maintainable realization. The six recommended revisions address: (1) latency and real-time processing bounds, (2) expert gate timeout and fallback behavior, (3) audit history retention and archival policy, (4) formalized interface contract between derivation engine and approval workflow, (5) fallback and circuit-breaker patterns for Safety Services unavailability, and (6) explicit traceability record schema. These revisions must be incorporated into DP-E before sub-FR decomposition begins, because they define the operational envelope and interface contracts that sub-FRs will depend on. Proceeding to sub-FRs without these revisions risks creating sub-level designs that violate latency constraints, create unbounded storage, or lack proper fault tolerance.

assumptions: Expert validation gates are acceptable latency-wise for the worksite mission context (assumption to be validated by REVISION 2 timeout policy definition).
Telemetry ingestion frequency and volume are compatible with Safety Services processing capacity (assumption to be validated by REVISION 1 latency SLA definition).
Audit trail retention requirements are compatible with Safety Services storage capacity (assumption to be validated by REVISION 3 retention policy definition).
Vehicle and Teleoperation modules can tolerate brief delays in performance target availability due to expert gate approval (assumption to be validated by REVISION 2 fallback behavior definition).
Safety Services is the appropriate single point of authority for performance target derivation and validation (validated by DP-IM gate; no override or bypass paths exist in Vehicle or Teleoperation).

Dual-layer traceability (automated + expert approval) is a sufficient and necessary governance pattern for safety-critical performance target derivation (validated by FR-DP gate; acceptance criteria explicitly require documented provenance).
open_risks: LATENCY SENSITIVITY (HIGH): If worksite context updates arrive at high frequency (e.g., >10 Hz) and performance target derivation must complete within hard real-time bounds (<100ms), Safety Services could become a bottleneck. Mitigation: Define latency SLA in REVISION 1; implement async buffering and batch processing if needed.

HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP DELAY (HIGH): Expert validation gates introduce non-deterministic approval latency. If performance targets must be applied within hard real-time bounds, gate approval could block critical path. Mitigation: Define gate timeout policy and conservative fallback targets in REVISION 2.

AUDIT HISTORY UNBOUNDED GROWTH (MEDIUM): SafetyAlert emission of every derivation step and approval decision could generate high-volume event stream. Without retention policy, Safety Services audit storage could become a data sink. Mitigation: Define retention windows and archival strategy in REVISION 3.

TIGHT COUPLING BETWEEN DERIVATION AND APPROVAL (MEDIUM): Dual-layer traceability requires synchronized state between automated parsing logic and expert approval state machine. Independent evolution of these components could increase maintenance burden and regression risk. Mitigation: Formalize interface contract in REVISION 4.

SINGLE POINT OF FAILURE (MEDIUM-HIGH): If Vehicle or Teleoperation modules depend on Safety Services for performance targets and Safety Services becomes unavailable, system could lose ability to derive and apply performance targets. Mitigation: Define fallback targets and circuit-breaker pattern in REVISION 5.

TRACEABILITY SCHEMA AMBIGUITY (MEDIUM): Acceptance criterion requires 'documented set of performance targets traceable to worksite inputs,' but traceability record structure is not formally specified. Ambiguity could lead to incomplete audit trails or verification failures. Mitigation: Formalize traceability record schema in REVISION 6.
evidence_needed: Worksite mission context update frequency and latency requirements (to validate REVISION 1 latency SLA).
Expert validation gate approval time distribution and acceptable timeout thresholds (to validate REVISION 2 timeout policy).
Audit trail event volume estimates and Safety Services storage capacity (to validate REVISION 3 retention policy).
Derivation engine algorithm versioning and approval workflow evolution roadmap (to validate REVISION 4 interface contract).
Vehicle and Teleoperation module dependency on Safety Services performance targets and fallback capability (to validate REVISION 5 circuit-breaker pattern).
Regulatory or compliance requirements for traceability record structure and audit trail completeness (to validate REVISION 6 schema formalization).
Real-time processing requirements and hard deadline constraints for performance target derivation and application (to validate overall DP-E feasibility under latency constraints).
